article.title: Microsoft Reveals MDASH, an Agentic Bug-Hunting Harness That Found 16 of May's Patch Tuesday Flaws and Tops the CyberGym Leaderboard
article.summary: Microsoft's Autonomous Code Security team disclosed MDASH, a multi-model scanning harness running over 100 agents, that identified 16 Windows networking and authentication bugs in May 2026 Patch Tuesday and scored 88.45 percent on CyberGym.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Microsoft used its May 2026 Patch Tuesday to publicly unveil MDASH, an internal agentic vulnerability discovery system that the company says found 16 of the bugs fixed in this month's release across the Windows networking and authentication stack, [according to the Microsoft Security Blog](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/05/12/defense-at-ai-speed-microsofts-new-multi-model-agentic-security-system-tops-leading-industry-benchmark/). The disclosure landed alongside one of the larger monthly patch cycles in recent memory — [120 CVE-numbered flaws by BleepingComputer's tally](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/microsoft/microsoft-may-2026-patch-tuesday-fixes-120-flaws-no-zero-days/) — and is paired with a limited private preview offering MDASH to a small set of customers.

## What We Know

Microsoft describes the system as a "multi-model agentic scanning harness (codename MDASH)" built by what it calls the Autonomous Code Security, or ACS, team, [according to its security blog](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/05/12/defense-at-ai-speed-microsofts-new-multi-model-agentic-security-system-tops-leading-industry-benchmark/). The same post notes that several ACS staffers "came to Microsoft from Team Atlanta," the group that won the DARPA AI Cyber Challenge.

Of the 16 bugs MDASH surfaced ahead of Patch Tuesday, Microsoft says "four Critical remote code execution flaws" sit "in components such as the Windows kernel TCP/IP stack and the IKEv2 service," [per the company](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/05/12/defense-at-ai-speed-microsofts-new-multi-model-agentic-security-system-tops-leading-industry-benchmark/). The discovery surfaces against a backdrop of unusually heavyweight critical CVEs in the same cycle: [The Hacker News flags](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/microsoft-patches-138-vulnerabilities.html) CVE-2026-41096, a CVSS 9.8 remote code execution flaw in the Windows DNS Client, and CVE-2026-41089, a CVSS 9.8 stack-based buffer overflow in Windows Netlogon, alongside CVE-2026-42898, a CVSS 9.9 code injection vulnerability in Microsoft Dynamics 365.

Architecturally, MDASH is built around an ensemble of language models that collectively run more than 100 specialized agents. Cyber Kendra describes the design as one in which "the system orchestrates over 100 specialized AI agents across an ensemble of frontier and distilled models, each assigned a distinct role: auditor, debater, deduplicator, or prover," [in its writeup](https://www.cyberkendra.com/2026/05/microsofts-ai-just-found-16-windows.html). The same article characterizes the workflow as adversarial, with one agent flagging a suspicious code path and another arguing against it before a finding is allowed to advance.

On the publicly graded CyberGym benchmark — which Microsoft says draws on 1,507 real-world vulnerabilities from 188 OSS-Fuzz projects — MDASH scored 88.45 percent, [according to the Microsoft Security Blog](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/05/12/defense-at-ai-speed-microsofts-new-multi-model-agentic-security-system-tops-leading-industry-benchmark/), claiming the top spot on the leaderboard by roughly five points. Microsoft also ran the system retroactively against five years of Microsoft Security Response Center cases in two heavily scrutinized Windows kernel components, reporting 96 percent recall on 28 cases in `clfs.sys` and 100 percent recall on 7 cases in `tcpip.sys`, [per the same post](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/05/12/defense-at-ai-speed-microsofts-new-multi-model-agentic-security-system-tops-leading-industry-benchmark/).

The May 2026 Patch Tuesday in which MDASH made its public debut shipped on May 12 in Redmond time, [as BleepingComputer reported](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/microsoft/microsoft-may-2026-patch-tuesday-fixes-120-flaws-no-zero-days/), with The Register tallying 137 CVEs and characterizing the cycle as one in which "none of which are known to have been targeted by attackers," [in its coverage](https://www.theregister.com/patches/2026/05/13/doozy-of-a-patch-tuesday-includes-30-critical-microsoft-cves/5239224). BleepingComputer counts 17 Critical-severity flaws this month, 14 of them remote code execution, [per its summary](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/microsoft/microsoft-may-2026-patch-tuesday-fixes-120-flaws-no-zero-days/). The Register notes that Microsoft is "making the tool available to a limited number of customers in private preview," [in the same writeup](https://www.theregister.com/patches/2026/05/13/doozy-of-a-patch-tuesday-includes-30-critical-microsoft-cves/5239224).

The two CVSS 9.8 networking flaws are drawing the most operational attention. For the DNS Client bug, Dustin Childs of Trend Micro's Zero Day Initiative told Help Net Security that "an attacker with a position to influence DNS responses could achieve unauthenticated RCE," [as reported by Help Net Security](https://www.helpnetsecurity.com/2026/05/12/microsoft-may-2026-patch-tuesday/). On the Netlogon side, Jason Kikta of Automox warned in the same article that "half-patched forests are not a defensible state for a pre-auth DC bug," [per Help Net Security](https://www.helpnetsecurity.com/2026/05/12/microsoft-may-2026-patch-tuesday/).

## What We Don't Know

Microsoft has not published the full list of the 16 CVEs that MDASH discovered, nor a per-bug attribution showing which agent roles surfaced which findings. The May 12 blog post names six Windows components MDASH probed — `tcpip.sys`, `ikeext.dll`, `http.sys`, `telnet.exe`, `netlogon.dll`, and `dnsapi.dll` — but does not specify which of this month's CVE numbers map to which file.

Pricing, eligibility, and rollout cadence for the private preview are also undisclosed. Microsoft says the system is being used internally by its own security engineering teams and tested by "a small set of customers," [per the security blog](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/05/12/defense-at-ai-speed-microsofts-new-multi-model-agentic-security-system-tops-leading-industry-benchmark/), but does not detail how the preview will scale or whether MDASH will be made generally available.

The count of May fixes itself differs across outlets — BleepingComputer's 120, The Register's 137, and The Hacker News's 138 — reflecting differences in how each tracker aggregates bundled, third-party, and re-release CVEs. None of the three reports any zero-days actively exploited at release.
